description: Giant, oddly-shaped open-ocean fish that visits cooler upwellings to be cleaned by bannerfish.
Smaller manta species commonly seen at cleaning stations and channels.
Largest ray species, seen in open water and at deep cleaning/feeding stations.
Seagrass-grazing marine mammal related to manatees, found in shallow tropical seagrass beds.
Largest fish in the ocean, a filter feeder often seen near plankton blooms and bagan fishing platforms.
Schooling shark species seen in current-swept channels and seamounts.
Flat, camouflaged carpet shark that rests motionless on reef bommies, ambushing passing prey.
Shark with an extremely long whip-like tail, used to stun small fish; visits seamount cleaning stations at dawn.
Small schooling baitfish that forms dense, swirling shoals near-shore, drawing in predators.
Bold, far-ranging open-ocean shark recognizable by its large white-tipped fins.
Stocky, adaptable shark tolerant of both salt and fresh water, often seen in baited dives.
Large, distinctively striped shark with a broad diet, known for curious but generally calm behaviour around divers.
The largest hammerhead species, identifiable by its tall, nearly straight-edged dorsal fin.
The smallest baleen whale, known in Australia for curious, voluntary interactions with snorkellers.
Large migratory baleen whale known for breaching and long, complex songs; calves and mates in warm tropical waters.
Common reef shark often seen patrolling current-swept channel walls in tight schools.
Docile-looking shark with protruding teeth, often found resting in caves and gullies on reef pinnacles.
Common sandy-bottom ray of the tropical Western Atlantic, often habituated to divers and snorkelers at feeding sites.
The largest toothed predator on Earth, a deep-diving whale recognizable by its massive block-shaped head.
Large herbivorous sea turtle that grazes on seagrass and algae on shallow reef flats.
Sea turtle with a narrow, pointed beak adapted for feeding on sponges among coral reefs.
The most commonly encountered reef shark in the Caribbean, often seen resting on the sand or patrolling reef walls.
Docile, bottom-dwelling shark frequently found resting motionless under ledges by day.
Graceful, polka-dotted ray often seen gliding over sand flats or leaping clear of the water.
Highly social, intelligent dolphin often found in pods riding currents through reef passes.
Slow-moving, spotted bottom-dwelling shark, juveniles are striped while adults are covered in dark spots.
Muck-dwelling octopus famous for impersonating the shape and movement of other venomous animals.
One of the world's largest stingray species, found gathering in caves and archways around temperate reefs.
The largest animal ever known to have lived, seen at the surface between deep dives while feeding on krill.
Iconic large predatory shark, often viewed from cages near seal colonies in cooler temperate waters.
Southern African fur seal that breeds in huge colonies on rocky islands, drawing in predators like great white sharks.
Large, curious North Atlantic seal that frequently approaches divers around its colony rocks.
Highly intelligent apex predator that travels in pods, sometimes following herring migrations into cold fjords.
Social, deep-diving whale that forms stable resident pods in a handful of locations worldwide.
Slender, vivid blue open-ocean shark often encountered on offshore seamount dives.
Fast-swimming relative of the manta ray, known for leaping and barrel-rolling at offshore feeding banks.
One of the world's rarest marine mammals, with a small protected population around Madeira's Desertas Islands.
Coastal baleen whale that comes remarkably close to shore in sheltered bays to calve and mate.
Playful, curious pinniped found in large colonies along the eastern Pacific coast.
Keystone kelp-forest species that keeps sea urchin populations in check; rafts often seen resting at the surface.
Enormous, slow-moving reef fish that gathers by the hundreds at a handful of wrecks each year to spawn.
Yellowish-brown shark that forms large winter aggregations along the Gulf Stream off southeast Florida.
Slender, fast-swimming open-ocean shark often seen schooling in the hundreds around offshore seamounts.
Small nocturnal shark able to "walk" across the reef flat on its fins, found in shallow Coral Triangle waters.
Rare, swirl-patterned frogfish that bounces along the seafloor rather than swimming.
Fur seal found around southern Australia and New Zealand, often curious and playful with divers.
Playful, endemic Galápagos pinniped, often found resting on beaches and lava rocks between swims.
The world's only sea-going lizard, found exclusively in the Galápagos, diving to graze on algae.
One of the world's rarest pinnipeds, found only along the southern Australian coastline.
Stocky fur seal found on rocky islands off southeastern Australia, often curious around divers.
Ornate, leaf-festooned relative of the seahorse found only in cool temperate southern Australian waters.
Cold-current penguin of the Peru-Chile coast, often seen diving for fish near rocky island colonies.
Antarctica's apex predator seal, powerful enough to hunt penguins, but also capable of curious, gentle encounters with divers.
Large, maned sea lion found along the coasts of Peru, Chile, and Argentina.
Acrobatic dolphin known for leaping and spinning along its body axis, often traveling in large pods.
The only penguin species found north of the equator, restricted entirely to the Galápagos Islands.
Galápagos-endemic cormorant that has lost the ability to fly, hunting instead by diving from rocky shores.
Migratory baleen whale famous for approaching small boats closely in its Baja California breeding lagoons.
Deep-water shark with six gill slits (most sharks have five), occasionally found in shallower cold water.
Large, sharp-toothed but docile eel-like fish that pairs for life in rocky North Pacific dens.
One of the rarest large whales on Earth, with only a few hundred individuals remaining.
The world's second-largest fish, a slow-moving plankton-feeder often seen filter-feeding at the surface.
The second-largest whale species on Earth, fast-swimming and often among the first migrants to arrive each season.
Small, colorful seabird that dives underwater to catch fish, nesting in large coastal colonies.
Cold-water relative of the short-finned pilot whale, found in large pods across the North Atlantic.
Small, common reef shark recognizable by black-tipped fins, often seen in very shallow water.
The largest and deepest-diving sea turtle, with a flexible leathery shell instead of a hard carapace.
Flat, ray-like shark that buries itself in sand; the Canary Islands are one of its last regular strongholds.
Large, curious Mediterranean grouper that often approaches divers fearlessly in protected reserves.
Large-headed sea turtle that nests on Mediterranean and subtropical beaches each summer.
Small, slender shark that rests motionless on the sand or in caves by day, common on Indo-Pacific reefs.
Tiny, camouflaged seahorse that lives almost exclusively on gorgonian sea fans, a prized macro-diving find.
Huge, long-lived reef fish with a distinctive humped forehead, often curious around divers.
Enormous parrotfish that travels in schools, using its bony forehead to graze on coral.
Long, snake-like reef fish that spends the day with just its head poking from a crevice, jaws agape.
Master of camouflage that ambushes prey using a lure-like fin, a favorite find for macro divers.
Brightly colored, shell-less sea slug found in an enormous variety of patterns on reefs worldwide.
Large, powerful reef shark with distinctive white-edged fins, often bolder than grey reef sharks around divers.
Long, torpedo-shaped predatory fish often seen schooling in dense, swirling "tornado" formations.
The world's largest and rarest stingray, regularly seen in open water only off Mozambique's Tofo Beach.
Small, patterned catshark endemic to South Africa, known for curling into a ring when disturbed.
Striped catshark endemic to South African waters, often seen resting in caves and crevices by day.
Large diving seabird that plunges from height to catch fish, nesting in dense coastal colonies.
Distinctive shark with seven gill slits (most sharks have five), found in cool coastal waters like Monterey Bay.
Bright orange damselfish and California's official state marine fish, often bold around divers.
Large-winged Pacific ray often seen partially buried in sand near kelp forests.
Flattened, camouflaged shark that lies partly buried on the seafloor, found off the California coast.
Small, vividly blue-spotted ray commonly found resting on sandy patches across Indo-Pacific reefs.
Tame, curious New Zealand reef fish that often swims right up to divers.
Rare, forest-nesting penguin endemic to New Zealand's remote southwest coastline.
Coastal seal found resting on rocks and feeding in cool temperate waters of the North Atlantic and Pacific.
Small, slender shark with spines in front of each dorsal fin, common in cool coastal waters.
Large electric ray capable of delivering a powerful shock, found on sandy bottoms in the Atlantic.
One of the largest stingray species in the Atlantic, with a long, spine-covered tail.
Small, harmless bottom-dwelling shark with a distinctive harness-like pattern, common in southern Australian waters.
Shy, deep-blue fish endemic to rocky reefs and caves of southeastern Australia.
Smallest and most abundant sea turtle species, named for its olive-colored heart-shaped shell.
Small, brightly-colored cuttlefish that flashes vivid pink, red and yellow patterns and walks along the seafloor.
Large, intelligent octopus found patrolling temperate reefs of southern Australia and New Zealand.
Small, shorthorned ray related to mantas, found in tropical Indo-Pacific waters.
Large flatfish found on cold North Atlantic seabeds, one of the largest bony fish species.
Large groundfish historically central to North Atlantic fisheries, found over rocky and sandy seabeds.
The largest living bivalve mollusk, found embedded in coral reefs across the Indo-Pacific.
Spiny-backed skate that spends most of its life on the seabed, found in coastal waters of the Northeast Atlantic.
Camouflage-skilled octopus with a single row of suckers, the only octopus species found in Scottish waters.
Ornate, leaf-camouflaged relative of the seahorse, found only in the temperate waters of southern Australia.
The smallest penguin species, breeding in burrows along the coastlines of southern Australia and New Zealand.
Rare seal endemic to the Hawaiian Islands, with roughly 1,400 individuals remaining in the wild.
Fast, acrobatic dolphin recognizable by its hourglass-patterned flanks, common across the Mediterranean.
Slender, spiny-maned seahorse found in seagrass meadows and rocky reefs of the eastern Atlantic.
Large, harmless requiem shark found around oceanic islands worldwide, growing up to 3 meters.
Small, shy cetacean found in cool coastal waters of the North Atlantic and North Pacific.
Large, sluggish bottom-dwelling shark found resting in caves and under ledges across the Indo-Pacific.
One of the smallest baleen whales, known for its curious nature and occasional close approaches to boats.
Large stingray with a distinctive fin-like fold along its tail, found on sandy seafloors of the Indo-Pacific and Red Sea.
Shark-like ray with a flattened, guitar-shaped body, found resting on sandy seafloors.
Large coastal raptor found hunting fish along the coastlines of Australia and South Asia.
Fish-hunting raptor found near coastlines and waterways on every continent except Antarctica.
Stocky shark with a tall, distinctive dorsal fin, found in coastal and offshore waters worldwide.
Small, slender nocturnal shark often found resting in crevices and under coral by day across Southeast Asia.
Large, inquisitive ray with a wingspan up to 3.7m, frequently making close approaches to divers around oceanic islands.
Fast-swimming spotted dolphin found in warm tropical and subtropical oceans worldwide, often in large pods.
Tiny, psychedelically-colored fish found in the rubble zones of sheltered lagoons and inshore reefs, active at dusk.
Large, fast pelagic tuna found in offshore tropical Indo-Pacific reef waters, a prized encounter for divers.
Fast-swimming penguin recognizable by its white eye stripe, found in large colonies on the Antarctic Peninsula and sub-Antarctic islands.
Fur seal found on rocky coastlines along the South American Pacific and Atlantic coasts.
Large black-and-white cormorant that nests in enormous colonies on Peruvian and Chilean islands, historically the world's primary source of guano.
Enormous spotted grouper named for its potato-like skin pattern, famous for approaching divers at the Cod Hole on Australia's Great Barrier Reef.
The world's largest octopus species, found in cold Pacific waters from Japan to Alaska and British Columbia.
Large black-and-white penguin nesting in vast colonies along the coasts of Argentina and Chile, including Peninsula Valdés.
Striking black-and-white dolphin endemic to Patagonian and Falkland Island waters, highly acrobatic in pods.
Large, slow-moving herbivorous marine mammal that grazes seagrass beds in shallow tropical Caribbean and Gulf of Mexico waters.
Medium-sized baleen whale found in warm tropical and subtropical seas, often seen lunging through baitballs during the sardine run.
Stocky, heavily scarred dolphin recognisable by its blunt head and grey-white body, favouring deep offshore canyons and oceanic islands.
Slender, uniformly dark whale that travels in tight social pods, known for offering food to human divers off Hawaii.
One of the fastest baleen whales, a seasonal migrant through oceanic island waters on its annual northward and southward passages.
Round-headed, beakless dolphin found in coastal and estuarine waters of South and Southeast Asia, capable of spitting water to herd fish.
Fast, energetic dolphin with a distinctive blue and white stripe, abundant in warm-temperate Atlantic and Mediterranean waters.
Africa's apex predator, living in cooperative prides and famous for spectacular hunts on open savanna.
World's largest land animal, living in matriarchal herds that travel vast distances across savanna and woodland.
Solitary, powerful big cat that hoists prey into trees; the most widely distributed of the large African cats.
World's second-largest land animal, a grazer that once ranged across Africa's grasslands; brought back from near-extinction by conservation efforts.
Smaller, hook-lipped browser; one of Africa's most endangered large mammals with fewer than 6,000 individuals remaining.
Semi-aquatic giant that spends the day submerged in rivers and lakes, emerging at dusk to graze on land.
World's fastest land animal, accelerating to 112 km/h in pursuit of prey across open African grasslands.
World's tallest animal, browsing acacia treetops across African savanna; recently reclassified as Vulnerable.
Most numerous and widespread African equid, travelling in vast herds on the annual Great Migration.
Gnu that conducts the world's largest overland migration — over 1.5 million animals moving between Serengeti and Masai Mara.
Africa's most successful predator by biomass; females lead clans and are larger than males in this highly social carnivore.
Most successful hunter in Africa by kill rate, operating in highly cooperative packs; fewer than 6,000 remain.
Massive bovine considered one of Africa's most dangerous animals; lives in large herds that defend members against lion attacks.
Critically rare great ape living only in the Virunga volcanoes and Bwindi forest; roughly 1,000 individuals remain.
Humans' closest living relative, using tools, hunting cooperatively, and living in complex social groups in African forests.
Widespread African wild pig that kneels to graze; runs with tail erect and retreats backwards into burrows.
Graceful African antelope with explosive leaping ability; one of the most abundant gazelles in East and Southern Africa.
Largest of all wild cats and India's national animal; fewer than 3,000 individuals survive, mainly in Indian forest reserves.
Slightly smaller than African relatives; lives in matriarchal herds across South and Southeast Asian forests.
World's fourth-largest land animal, inhabiting the tall grass floodplains of Nepal and Assam; single horn can reach 50 cm.
Elusive high-altitude big cat of Central and South Asian mountains; uses its long thick tail for balance on rocky terrain.
Iconic black-and-white bear endemic to mountain bamboo forests in southwest China; a symbol of global conservation.
World's largest arboreal mammal, spending almost all its life in the rainforest canopy of Borneo.
Smallest surviving tiger subspecies, restricted to Sumatra's remaining rainforests; fewer than 400 individuals remain.
Distinctive black-and-white tapir of Southeast Asian rainforests; an ancient species little changed for millions of years.
World's smallest bear, with a distinctive chest patch and very long tongue for extracting honey from beehives in SE Asian forests.
Bizarre-nosed monkey endemic to Borneo's coastal and riverine mangrove forests, an excellent swimmer.
Smaller than African subspecies with a distinct belly fold; fewer than 700 survive, all in Gujarat's Gir Forest.
Largest Asian deer, the primary prey of Bengal tigers in most Indian forest reserves.
World's largest wild cattle species, standing up to 2.2 m at the shoulder; found in South and Southeast Asian forests.
Russet-furred arboreal mammal of Himalayan bamboo forests; despite its name it is not related to giant pandas.
North American brown bear subspecies, keystone predator of mountain and coastal ecosystems.
Apex predator that rebalances entire ecosystems through trophic cascades; reintroduced to Yellowstone in 1995.
North America's largest land animal; once roamed the continent in tens of millions before near-extermination in the 19th century.
Second-largest member of the deer family, famed for its autumn bugling rut in North American mountain parks.
World's largest deer species, browsing boreal wetlands and willow thickets across the northern hemisphere.
Reindeer of the tundra and boreal forest, undertaking some of the longest land migrations on Earth.
Most common bear species in North America; highly adaptable, ranging from deep forest to suburban edges.
Americas' most widely distributed wild cat, ranging from Yukon to Patagonia; known as cougar, puma, or panther regionally.
North America's fastest land animal and the second-fastest in the world, reaching 88 km/h on open prairies.
Rocky Mountain wild sheep famous for the head-clashing battles of males during the autumn rut.
The most abundant large wild mammal in the Americas, adaptable to forest, farmland, and suburban habitats.
Highly adaptable North American canid that has expanded its range dramatically as wolves declined.
Americas' only big cat; the largest in the Western Hemisphere, a powerful swimmer that hunts caimans and capybaras.
Largest native land mammal of South America, an important seed disperser in Amazonian and Atlantic forests.
Iconic South American insectivore with a 60 cm tongue, consuming 35,000 ants and termites daily.
World's largest rodent, a semi-aquatic herd animal found in South American wetlands and riverbanks.
Long-legged South American canid of the cerrado grassland; its red coat and tall legs allow it to see over tall grass.
South America's only bear species, a forest dweller that builds platforms in trees to feed on bromeliads and fruit.
World's longest otter at up to 1.8 m, living in family groups along South American river systems.
Wild ancestor of the domestic llama, roaming Patagonian steppe in herds; primary prey of the puma in South America.
Large, acrobatic Amazonian primate that uses its prehensile tail as a fifth limb when swinging through the canopy.
Europe's largest land predator; populations persist in the Carpathians, Balkans, and Scandinavian forests.
Europe's heaviest land animal, saved from extinction in the 1920s through captive breeding and reintroduction.
Europe and Asia's largest lynx species; a solitary, tufted-eared predator of roe deer and chamois in boreal and mountain forests.
World's most endangered wild cat, recovering slowly in Spain and Portugal after plunging to just 100 individuals in 2002.
Wild mountain goat of the Alps, saved from extinction by Victor Emmanuel II's royal hunting reserve in Gran Paradiso.
Europe's largest native deer species, with spectacular antlered stags roaring during the autumn rut.
Agile mountain goat-antelope of European mountain ranges, bounding across near-vertical cliffs with ease.
The largest terrestrial mustelid, a ferocious Scandinavian and boreal predator capable of killing prey many times its size.
World's largest marsupial and Australia's largest native land animal, reaching 2 m tall in outback grasslands.
Iconic arboreal marsupial that sleeps up to 22 hours a day on a highly toxic eucalyptus leaf diet.
Africa's largest reptile and apex aquatic predator, ambushing wildebeest and zebra crossing rivers on the Great Migration.
Conservation success story of the US Everglades; a keystone species whose "gator holes" support entire wetland ecosystems.
World's largest living reptile, reaching 6 m; found from northern Australia to Southeast Asia in coastal rivers and estuaries.
World's largest living lizard, endemic to a few Indonesian islands where it ambushes deer and buffalo with toxic saliva.
World's largest tortoise, living over 100 years on the Galápagos Islands; different island populations have distinct shell shapes.
Most abundant crocodilian in the world, with millions inhabiting the Pantanal and surrounding South American wetlands.
Stocky, yellow-hued iguana endemic to the Galápagos, living in a mutualistic relationship with Darwin's finches for tick removal.
First species to be declared extinct in the wild (1972) and then successfully reintroduced; a symbol of Middle Eastern conservation.
Madagascar's most recognisable primate, living in social troops and using its bold striped tail for group communication.
Largest living lemur, known for its haunting territorial calls that echo through eastern Madagascar's rainforest.
World's largest bird and fastest two-legged runner, able to sustain 50 km/h across African savanna.
Tallest flamingo species, gathering in spectacular flocks of thousands at shallow alkaline lakes in Africa and Europe.
National symbol of the United States; a powerful fish hunter that recovered from near-extinction after DDT was banned.
Largest and most powerful eagle in the Americas, hunting monkeys and sloths in the Amazon rainforest canopy.
World's largest flying land bird by combined wingspan and weight, soaring on Andean thermals for hours without flapping.
Iconic large parrot of Central and South American forests, living in pairs and flocks that fly noisily over the canopy.
Largest toucan species with a huge colourful bill used to reach fruit; found in South American forest edges and cerrado.
World's most dangerous bird — a large, flightless rainforest dweller of Australia and New Guinea with a bony casque and dagger-clawed feet.
Australia's largest bird and world's second-tallest, undertaking seasonal migrations across the continent's interior.
Long-legged African raptor that hunts on foot, killing snakes and lizards with powerful stamping strikes.
Galápagos icon famous for its vivid blue feet, which males display in an elaborate high-stepping courtship dance.
New Zealand's national icon — a flightless, nocturnal bird with nostrils at the tip of its beak for finding earthworms.
World's only alpine parrot, found in New Zealand's Southern Alps; renowned for intelligence and mischievous behaviour.
Stunning pink wading bird of the Americas, swinging its spoon-shaped bill through shallow water to filter small crustaceans.
Iconic desert antelope of the Namib and Kalahari; long straight horns and black-and-white face mask make it unmistakeable.
South Africa's national animal; forms vast aggregating herds in the Etosha pan, famous for its bounding pronking leaps.
Namibian endemic subspecies of impala with a distinctive dark blaze on the face; restricted to north-west Namibia and southern Angola.
Magnificent antelope of miombo woodland; bulls are jet black with sweeping scimitar horns up to 165 cm long.
World's largest antelope; surprisingly agile for its bulk, capable of clearing 1.5-metre fences; found across eastern and southern Africa.
Zambian near-endemic antelope found in floodplain grasslands; closely related to the kob; a speciality of South Luangwa and Kafue.
Semi-aquatic antelope endemic to the Kafue Flats of Zambia; grazes in the flooded grasslands and flees predators by wading into water.
World's largest antelope subspecies; restricted to a few protected areas in West Africa including Niokolo-Koba; fewer than 200 remain.
Most endangered large mammal on Earth; fewer than 80 survive exclusively in Ujung Kulon NP, Java — the last population anywhere.
Smallest and most ancient rhino species; hairy and two-horned; fewer than 80 survive in Sumatran and Bornean rainforest fragments.
Wild cattle of Southeast Asia; bulls are deep blue-black, cows and calves tawny; found in Ujung Kulon and Way Kambas in significant numbers.
World's smallest pig; once widespread across the Himalayan foothills terai; now restricted to a tiny relict population in Manas NP, Assam.
Magnificent golden-coated leaf monkey endemic to a sliver of Assam and Bhutan; stronghold in Manas NP along the Manas River.
Indian swamp deer or hard-ground barasingha; endemic to India; the Kanha subspecies was brought back from near-extinction by conservation efforts.
North America's largest flying bird with a 3-metre wingspan; extinct in the wild in 1987, brought back by captive breeding; now soars over the Sierra Nevada.
North America's heaviest native bird; reintroduced to Grand Teton and Yellowstone; bugling calls carry across the glacial lakes at dawn.
World's largest flying parrot; brilliant cobalt-blue; stronghold in the Brazilian cerrado and Pantanal, nesting in ancient manduvi trees.
Dazzling orange bird of Guiana highland rainforest; males gather at leks on mossy boulders to perform extraordinary dancing displays.
South America's largest deer; semi-aquatic, splayed hooves prevent sinking; a signature species of the Ibera marshes and Pantanal wetlands.
South America's largest armadillo, up to 1.5 m long; nocturnal, fossorial, and cryptic; specialist on termite and ant nests in cerrado and forest.
Stocky, short-legged pack hunter of South American forests; semi-aquatic, webbed feet, and hunts prey larger than itself as a coordinated group.
One of the world's least-known wild cats; found only in the high Andes above 3,500 m; preys almost exclusively on mountain viscachas.
Slender wild camelid of the high Andes; produces the world's finest natural fibre; recovered from near-extinction to hundreds of thousands.
Largest predator in South America; endemic to the Orinoco basin; reduced to perhaps 1,500 individuals; a critically endangered freshwater giant.
One of only five living monotremes; lays eggs but nurses young on milk secreted through patches; common across Australia in many habitats.
Extraordinary egg-laying mammal with a duck bill, beaver tail, and otter feet; males have venomous ankle spurs; hunts by electroreception.
Small wallaby native to south-west Australia; famous as "the world's happiest animal" due to its upturned mouth; stronghold on Rottnest Island.
Australia's only arboreal kangaroo; lives in the rainforest canopy of the Wet Tropics, Queensland; a reclusive foliage-eater.
Golf-ball-sized octopus of Indo-Pacific rock pools and reefs; iridescent blue rings pulse when threatened; venom can kill a human in minutes.
Living fossil unchanged for 500 million years; emerges from depth at night to hunt crustaceans; treasured shell harvested to near-extinction in parts of its range.
Strikingly banded black-and-white sea snake of the Indo-Pacific; breathes air but hunts eels underwater; docile and regularly encountered by divers.
Juvenile whale sharks gather in predictable aggregations at Ningaloo Reef and Oslob; these filter-feeders are the centrepiece of snorkel tourism.
Uses its extraordinarily long upper tail lobe to stun schooling fish; cleaned at seamount cleaning stations in the Philippines and Maldives.
Flamboyant moray of Indo-Pacific reefs; juveniles are jet black, adult males electric blue, females yellow; all individuals start as males.
World's most endangered canid and Africa's rarest carnivore; endemic to Ethiopia's afro-alpine moorlands where it specialises in hunting giant mole rats.
Ethiopia's bleeding-heart monkey; the only grass-eating primate, living in huge troops on high escarpments — the most terrestrial of all primates.
Magnificent spiral-horned antelope endemic to Ethiopia's highland forests; larger than greater kudu; restricted to the Bale Mountains and surrounds.
Critically rare ibex found only on Simien Mountains' northern escarpment; one of the world's rarest animals with fewer than 1,000 individuals.
Vivid orange-and-black monkey endemic to the Albertine Rift highlands; shares its bamboo forest habitat with mountain gorillas in Volcanoes NP and Virunga.
Prehistoric-looking giant of papyrus swamps, with a massive shoe-shaped bill that snaps up lungfish; stands motionless for hours before an explosive strike.
Turkey-sized, red-faced hornbill that strides across the African savanna hunting snakes and lizards; groups have complex cooperative breeding systems.
Africa's heaviest flying bird, weighing up to 19 kg; males inflate their neck sacs in dramatic booming displays on open African grassland.
Largest living primate subspecies; inhabits Central African rainforest; more secretive than its mountain counterpart, with a brownish-grey coat.
World's largest monkey and arguably the most colourful mammal; males' vivid blue-and-red faces are unique; lives in super-troops of hundreds in Central African rainforest.
Semi-aquatic spiral-horned antelope of African wetlands; splayed hooves prevent sinking; flees into water to escape predators and wades chest-deep to feed.
Chestnut-coloured antelope of southern African floodplains; specially adapted for wading; herds of thousands run through shallow water in spectacular fashion.
World's largest wild equid; narrowly-striped and donkey-eared; endangered and restricted to northern Kenya and Ethiopia — distinct from the common Plains Zebra.
Most visually striking giraffe species; large polygonal patches separated by crisp white lines; restricted to Somalia, Ethiopia, and northern Kenya.
The giraffe-necked gazelle; stands upright on its hind legs to browse acacia leaves no other antelope can reach; found only in arid East Africa.
South Africa's only penguin species, also called the jackass penguin for its braying call; declining sharply due to overfishing of its prey species.
Madagascar's apex predator; cat-like but more closely related to mongooses; climbs trees with semi-retractable claws to ambush lemurs.
Pure-white dancing lemur of southern Madagascar; crosses open ground in sideways bounding leaps on its hind legs — one of nature's most spectacular locomotion displays.
Madagascar's strangest primate; has an elongated skeletal middle finger for tapping on wood to locate and extract grubs — the ecological equivalent of a woodpecker.
Genetically isolated Apennine subspecies with only ~50 individuals; saved from extinction by Abruzzo NP; completely disjunct from other European bear populations.
One of the world's rarest raptors; endemic to the Iberian Peninsula with fewer than 1,000 individuals; a flagship of conservation in Doñana NP.
Norway's last truly wild reindeer herds survive in mountain national parks; genetically distinct from semi-domesticated populations and critically important to conserve.
Turkey-sized grouse of ancient boreal and montane forests; males perform spectacular lekking displays in Scotland's Cairngorms and Scandinavian forests at dawn.
Perhaps the most beautiful bird in the Americas; males trail 60 cm iridescent tail plumes; sacred to the Maya and Aztec as symbol of freedom; lives in cloud forest.
World's heaviest eagle, up to 9 kg; massive yellow bill and dark plumage with white shoulder patches; winters in Hokkaido, Japan, where it fishes through sea ice.
Also known as the chiru; endemic to the Tibetan Plateau, migrating in their thousands to calve in summer; their fine underfur (shahtoosh) is one of the finest animal fibres.
Smallest and gentlest of Asian elephants, unique to northern Borneo; rounder face, larger ears relative to body, and notably tamer behaviour than mainland Asian elephants.
Island-endemic leopard subspecies; with no other large cats competing, it has assumed the role of apex predator; Yala NP has the world's highest density of wild leopards.
Semi-aquatic wildcat of South and Southeast Asian wetlands; swims readily and scoops fish with its slightly webbed front paws; found in mangroves and riverine forest.
North America's most endangered mammal; declared extinct in the wild in 1987 but reintroduced from captive breeding; hunts prairie dogs in their own burrows.
North America's tallest bird and most dramatic conservation story; down to 21 individuals in 1941, now around 800 in the wild; nests only in Wood Buffalo NP, Canada.
One of the world's most endangered large cats; fewer than 200 survive in southern Florida's swamps; a subspecies of mountain lion genetically isolated for millennia.
Gentle aquatic herbivore of Florida's warm springs and Caribbean coasts; one of the USA's most beloved conservation icons, scarred by boat propellers.
Pure-white wild sheep of subarctic North America; rams' massive curling horns can weigh more than the rest of the skeleton combined; inhabits the most extreme mountain terrain.
Patagonia's endemic deer and national animal of Chile; a stocky, grey-brown deer of remote Andean valleys; fewer than 2,000 survive and populations are declining.
South America's tallest flying bird, standing 1.4 metres; the flagship species of the Pantanal; hunts fish, frogs, and snakes with its massive upturned bill.
Barrel-shaped Australian burrowing marsupial that produces cubic faeces — the only animal known to do so; a powerful digger with backwards-facing pouch.
World's largest carnivorous marsupial; found only in Tasmania; its shrieking calls and ferocious feeding frenzies earned its fearsome name; threatened by facial tumour disease.
New Zealand's endemic penguin and one of the world's rarest; named for its distinctive pale yellow eye-ring; nests in coastal forest and scrub rather than colonies.
World's smallest and rarest dolphin, endemic to New Zealand coastal waters; distinctive round dorsal fin and black-white-grey patterning; fewer than 15,000 remain.

Critically endangered great ape found only in the rainforests of northern Sumatra; more social and slightly smaller than its Bornean relative, sharing Gunung Leuser's forest with tigers, rhinos, and elephants.
Bhutan's national animal, a goat-antelope with a moose-like face found in Eastern Himalayan bamboo forests; herds move to high alpine meadows in summer and descend to warmer valleys in winter.
Adaptable big cat found throughout the Indian subcontinent's forests, sharing reserves with tigers by hunting more at night and favouring rockier, thicker terrain.
Distinct tiger subspecies found only in Peninsular Malaysia's rainforests; fewer than 150 remain in the wild, with Taman Negara among its last strongholds.
The largest leopard subspecies, found in rocky, forested terrain from the Caucasus through Iran; Golestan NP holds one of its most significant remaining populations.
Massive wild ancestor of the domestic water buffalo; Kaziranga holds the large majority of the world's remaining genetically pure wild population, grazing the park's floodplain grasslands.
China-endemic primate with a vivid blue face and golden-orange coat; lives in cold, high-altitude conifer forest across several isolated Sichuan, Shaanxi, Gansu, and Hubei mountain populations.
Golden-brown goat-antelope of Sichuan's bamboo forests, moving in herds between high summer pastures and sheltered winter valleys.
Striking golden-coated takin subspecies restricted to the Qinling Mountains of Shaanxi; named for the 11th Duke of Bedford, who helped save the species from extinction.
One of the world's rarest birds a generation ago; rediscovered as just seven individuals in Yangxian, Shaanxi in 1981, with the entire wild population descending from that single flock.
World's largest amphibian, reaching 1.8 m; a living fossil little changed in 170 million years, found in cold, clear mountain streams across central and southern China.
Strikingly patterned long-tailed pheasant found only in the hill forests of southeastern China.
Long-tailed pheasant endemic to central China's mountain forests, the male's tail the longest of any pheasant species at up to 1.5 metres.
Vividly-coloured pheasant endemic to the mountains of southeastern China; males display inflatable blue-and-red throat lappets during courtship.
Iridescent high-altitude pheasant found only in the alpine scrub and meadow of Sichuan, Gansu, and Shaanxi.
Forest-dwelling bear with a distinctive pale chest crescent, ranging from the Himalaya through China and mainland Southeast Asia to Japan.
Secretive forest cat with the longest canine teeth relative to skull size of any living cat; an agile climber of South and Southeast Asian rainforest.
Medium-sized wildcat of South and Southeast Asian forest, with a coat ranging from golden-brown to grey.
Southeast Asia's largest and most colourful pheasant; far rarer than its Indian cousin and increasingly restricted to a handful of protected river valleys.
Small, secretive barking deer native to the forests of southern and eastern China, now also introduced to parts of Britain and Ireland.
One of the world's largest and most widespread raptors, hunting marmots and hares over open mountains and steppe across the Northern Hemisphere.
Large fish- and waterbird-hunting eagle of Eurasian wetlands and coasts, recolonising parts of its former range in recent years.
Ornately-patterned East Asian duck; the males' iridescent plumage is among the most striking in the bird world, breeding in wetlands and forest pools across China, Korea, and Japan.
Large grey goose whose wild population winters overwhelmingly at a single Chinese lake complex, grazing exposed lakebed vegetation in enormous flocks.
World's second-rarest crane and a symbol of longevity in East Asian culture; breeds in northeast Chinese and Russian marshes with a striking red crown patch.
Elegant grey crane breeding in northeast Asian wetlands and wintering in flocks on East Asian floodplains and estuaries.
Snow-white crane whose entire eastern population — over 95% of the world's total — winters at a single Chinese lake.
Rare black-and-white stork of East Asian wetlands, nesting in tall trees and pylons near shallow lakes and floodplains.
Small freshwater porpoise endemic to the Yangtze River system; a handful of large lakes hold its last significant populations following the extinction of the Yangtze river dolphin.
World's largest cat, adapted to cold temperate forest; the Russia-China border population is slowly recovering and expanding westward.
Small, sure-footed goat-antelope of steep forested slopes in the Russian Far East, northeast China, and Korea.

Black-and-white snub-nosed monkey found only in a narrow band of high-altitude conifer forest between the Yangtze and Mekong rivers; feeds almost entirely on lichen, one of the highest-altitude primates on Earth.
Small, fanged deer of high-altitude Asian forest and scrub, hunted historically for its musk gland; found from the Himalaya across the Tibetan Plateau's eastern fringes.
Genetically distinct wild ancestor of the domestic Bactrian camel; fewer than 1,000 survive in the remotest deserts of the China-Mongolia border region.
Massive, shaggy wild ancestor of the domestic yak, grazing the highest and coldest grasslands on Earth across the Tibetan Plateau.
Largest of the Asiatic wild asses, forming herds of dozens on the open steppe of the Tibetan Plateau above 4,000 m.
Small, white-rumped gazelle endemic to the Tibetan Plateau, sharing its high grassland habitat with chiru and wild yak.
Also called Thorold's deer; a large, pale-muzzled deer endemic to the highest alpine meadows fringing the Tibetan Plateau.
Large burrowing rodent of high-altitude Himalayan and Tibetan grassland, a key prey species for snow leopards and golden eagles.
Goat-antelope of the high Himalaya and Tibetan Plateau; its grey-blue coat provides camouflage on rocky slopes, and it is the principal prey of the snow leopard.
Small, brightly-coloured marmot endemic to the Western Tian Shan of Uzbekistan and Kazakhstan, living colonially on steep alpine slopes.
World's largest wild sheep, found across the high mountains and steppe of Central Asia; several regional subspecies are recognised, including the giant-horned Marco Polo sheep.
Argali subspecies named for the explorer who first described it in 1271; males carry spiralling horns up to 190 cm, the largest of any wild sheep, restricted to the high Pamir plateau.
Central Asia's largest wild goat, with backswept ridged horns exceeding a metre; scales sheer cliffs of the Tian Shan, Altai, and Pamir ranges.
Reddish wild sheep of Central and South Asian hills, smaller than the argali, with males carrying a distinctive dark neck ruff.
Desert and steppe gazelle named for the male's throat swelling during rut, ranging from the Arabian Peninsula across Central Asia to Mongolia.
Central Asian subspecies of the Asiatic wild ass, once reduced to a single Turkmenian population before reintroduction to reserves across Kazakhstan and Mongolia.
Endangered red deer subspecies restricted to riverine tugai forest along a handful of Central Asian rivers in Uzbekistan, Tajikistan, and Turkmenistan.
Bizarre-nosed steppe antelope that undertakes mass migrations across Kazakhstan; the population crashed by over 90% in a 2015 die-off but is recovering under strict protection.
Steppe gazelle that forms some of the largest remaining ungulate migrations on Earth, with herds of tens of thousands moving across the Mongolian and Chinese grassland.
The world's last truly wild horse species, extinct in the wild by 1969 and rebuilt entirely from zoo-bred stock; now free-ranging again across several Mongolian reserves.
World's rarest bear subspecies, with as few as 30-40 individuals surviving in a single strictly protected area of the Mongolian Gobi Desert.
Stocky, dense-furred wildcat of Central Asian steppe and mountain, with the longest fur of any cat species and a permanently grumpy expression.
Small steppe fox of Central Asia, hunting rodents and birds across open grassland and semi-desert; some populations move seasonally in loose groups.
World's largest pelican species and heaviest flying bird, breeding in colonies at Central Asian and Balkan wetlands.
Wide-ranging raptor of the Eurasian steppe, nesting on the ground and preying heavily on susliks and other burrowing rodents.
Large eagle of open steppe and woodland edge from Central Europe to Central Asia, nesting in isolated trees overlooking grassland hunting grounds.
One of the world's highest-flying birds, crossing the Himalaya at altitudes above 7,000 m during migration; breeds at high-altitude lakes across the Tibetan Plateau and Central Asia.
World's most endangered big cat subspecies, with as few as 30 individuals surviving, all in the arid basins of central Iran.
Iranian subspecies of the Asiatic wild ass, confined to a handful of protected desert basins in central Iran, with a small reintroduced population in Israel.
Middle Eastern deer once thought extinct in the wild by the 1950s; rediscovered in Iran and rebuilt through captive breeding, now reintroduced to reserves in Israel and Iran.
Smallest leopard subspecies, adapted to the arid mountains of the Arabian Peninsula; fewer than 200 survive in the wild.
Small goat-antelope endemic to the Hajar Mountains of Oman and the UAE, the region's only surviving population of true tahr outside the Himalaya.
Desert-adapted wild goat of rocky escarpments from the Sahara through Sinai and Arabia, with dramatically long backswept horns in males.
Small mountain gazelle of the Arabian Peninsula, reintroduced to several protected areas after near-disappearance from unregulated hunting.
Pale, sand-coloured gazelle of the Arabian desert interior, among the most heat-tolerant of all gazelle species.
Small, heat-tolerant gazelle of North African and Middle Eastern deserts, able to survive without drinking water for extended periods by extracting moisture from plants.
Highly social primate of the Horn of Africa and southwestern Arabia, living in complex multi-tiered troops of up to several hundred individuals.
Small, wide-eared desert cat able to survive without drinking free water, ranging from the Sahara through Arabia to Central Asia.
Athletic, tufted-eared wildcat capable of leaping more than 3 m to strike birds from the air, found across Africa, the Middle East, and Central Asia.
Small, solitary wild relative of the domestic cat, found across Europe, Africa, and the Middle East in forest, scrub, and mountain habitat.
Small, long-eared fox of rocky mountain terrain from the Middle East to Central Asia, an agile climber able to scale near-vertical rock faces.
Adaptable, vocal canid of the Middle East, southeast Europe, and South Asia, thriving in wetlands, farmland, and scrub.
Solitary, nocturnal scavenger found from North Africa through the Middle East to Central Asia and India; Lebanon's national animal.
Guinea-pig-sized mammal whose closest living relatives are elephants and manatees; lives in colonies among rocky outcrops across Africa and the Middle East.
South and Southeast Asia's largest otter species; a small, geographically isolated population known as Maxwell's otter survives only in Iraq's Mesopotamian marshes.
Small, white scavenging vulture found from Iberia to India; famously one of the few birds known to use tools, cracking ostrich eggs with thrown stones.
Small migratory songbird that breeds almost exclusively in the reedbeds of the Iraqi and Iranian Mesopotamian marshes before wintering in East Africa.
Pale, delicately-patterned duck of Mediterranean and Middle Eastern marshes, with a global stronghold in Iraq's Mesopotamian wetlands.
Iridescent nectar-feeding bird found only on Socotra and its satellite islands, in scrubland and open woodland.
Glossy dark starling endemic to the Socotra archipelago, often seen foraging around cliffs and villages.
Small, drab warbler endemic to Socotra's scrubland and semi-desert, one of the island's ten endemic bird species.
Slow-moving, colour-changing lizard found only on the Socotra archipelago, among the roughly 90% of the island's reptile species found nowhere else.
Small, elegant deer of European and Central Asian woodland and forest-steppe, males growing short, upright antlers each year.
Adaptable, tusked wild pig found across forest, steppe, and semi-desert habitat from Europe through Central Asia to East Asia.
Small, fanged, hornless deer of Siberian and Mongolian taiga forest, hunted historically for its musk gland.
Stiff-tailed diving duck with a swollen sky-blue bill in breeding males; Central Asian lakes hold some of its most important wintering populations.

Corkscrew-horned wild goat restricted to a handful of mountain ranges on the Tajikistan-Afghanistan border; recovering steadily under community-based conservation after falling to a few hundred animals.
Central Asia and North Africa's largest lizard, reaching up to 1.5 m; a formidable desert predator of rodents, reptiles, and eggs.
Large, powerful falcon of Central Asian and Eastern European steppe, prized in falconry and declining sharply from illegal trapping.
Shy, dark-plumaged relative of the white stork, nesting in remote forest and cliffs across Europe and Asia rather than near people.
Large, trumpeting wild swan of northern Eurasia, breeding on remote lakes and wintering in large flocks further south.
Large, quill-covered rodent found from the Middle East through Central Asia to South Asia, sheltering by day in burrows and rock crevices.
Venomous cobra of Central Asian deserts, semi-desert, and foothills, distinguished from its South Asian relatives by its lack of a hood pattern.
Stocky, burrowing mustelid found across Europe and Central Asia, living in large family groups called setts.
Wild ancestor of the domestic goat, found on steep, rocky mountains from the Caucasus and Kopet Dag through the Zagros; males carry long scimitar-shaped horns.
Europe and North Africa's only wild monkey, now restricted to fragmented cedar and oak forest in Morocco and Algeria's Atlas and Rif mountains.
North Africa's only native deer, a red deer subspecies restricted to a handful of cedar and oak forests in Algeria and Tunisia, with a reintroduced population in Morocco.
Shaggy-throated wild sheep of North Africa's mountains and rocky desert, remarkably heat-tolerant and capable of surviving on minimal water.
Pale, desert-adapted cheetah subspecies scattered thinly across the central Sahara; with perhaps 250 individuals left, it is one of the rarest large cats on Earth.
Deep chestnut-brown diving duck of Eurasian and North African wetlands, declining across much of its range from habitat loss.
The largest living primate, found only in eastern DR Congo's lowland and montane forest; numbers have collapsed by more than 75% since the 1990s due to conflict and mining.
Giraffe relative with zebra-striped legs, found only in the dense rainforest of the Democratic Republic of Congo; so elusive it was unknown to science until 1901.
Smaller, straighter-tusked relative of the savanna elephant, found in the dense rainforest of the Congo Basin; gathers by the hundreds at forest clearings called bais to drink mineral-rich water.
Largest and most colourful African forest antelope, with a rich chestnut coat and bold white stripes; shy and rarely seen in the dense Congo Basin rainforest it inhabits.
Reclusive, largely nocturnal relative of the hippopotamus, a fraction of its cousin's size; restricted to a handful of West African rainforest rivers and swamps.
Small, drab-coloured colobus monkey of West African forest, the only colobus species in which mothers carry infants in their mouths rather than on their backs.
Largest forest duiker species, with a striking grey-and-black coat; among the rarest antelopes on Earth, restricted to a few Upper Guinea rainforest fragments.
Reddish-brown antelope of West and Central African floodplains and savanna, males gathering at small territorial leks to attract females.
Long-faced, oddly-proportioned antelope of African savanna, among the fastest antelope species over long distances despite its ungainly appearance.
One of Africa's largest antelope species, with a horse-like face, backswept horns, and distinctive black-and-white facial markings.
Shaggy-coated antelope always found near water, secreting an oily, musky substance that helps waterproof its coat and may deter predators.
Tallest gazelle species, once widespread across the Sahara and Sahel; reduced to a few hundred individuals in scattered, fragmented desert populations.
Large, ground-dwelling bird of the Sahara's southern fringe and Sahel, declining sharply from hunting and habitat degradation.
World's smallest canid, instantly recognisable by its huge ears that radiate heat and detect prey moving underground in the Sahara's sand.
Shaggy-maned scavenger of southern Africa's deserts and semi-desert, able to travel vast distances alone each night in search of carrion.
Adaptable, silver-backed canid of eastern and southern Africa, an opportunistic scavenger and hunter often seen trotting the fringes of predator kills.
Narrow-striped zebra subspecies adapted to the steep, rocky escarpments of Namibia and southwestern Angola, with a distinctive dewlap on the throat.
Kob subspecies of the South Sudanese wetlands, at the heart of the largest land mammal migration on Earth — millions move seasonally across the Boma-Badingilo landscape.
East African topi subspecies found in huge herds across South Sudan's floodplains, migrating alongside white-eared kob in one of the world's great wildlife spectacles.
Striking amphibious antelope restricted to the Sudd and Gambella wetlands of South Sudan and Ethiopia; males turn glossy black-and-white in breeding condition.
Grassland gazelle restricted to South Sudan's floodplains, part of the great white-eared kob migration system.
Sturdy, short-horned antelope of African floodplains and reedbeds, usually found in pairs or small family groups near water.
